citation: Varsa, J. (2022). Oconee County Library puts on inaugural FanFest. Georgia Library Quarterly, 59(3), 1-2.
summary:
Varsa provides a short practitioner report on the first FanFest hosted by Oconee County Library, a branch of the Athens Regional Library System in Georgia. The event took place on April 2, 2022, at the Oconee County Civic Center in Watkinsville and was supported by the Oconee County Library Friends, staff, and volunteers. It was admission-free, lasted a full day, drew more than 350 participants, and was framed as a community celebration in the tradition of comic cons.

The article defines FanFest as an all-ages festival that brings together live music, guest authors and artists, a cosplay contest, gaming, vendors, food, and other activities. Its distinguishing feature is localism. Rather than building a program only around nationally recognizable franchises or commercial fandom culture, the library used the event to showcase local authors, musicians, gaming organizations, vendors, food trucks, student volunteers, and community partners. Varsa notes that many participating organizations were already library-system partners and represented the diversity of northeast Georgia.

The service model is spatially and organizationally broader than a single in-library program. Oconee County Library used a partner venue, the Oconee County Civic Center, from 9:30 a.m. to 5:30 p.m. The main hall held vendors who could display books and products while talking with attendees. The auditorium hosted three music acts, while the lawn supported food trucks and sword demonstrations from the Athens School of Arms. The program also included workshops, panel discussions, book signings, live music, and other activities.

Gaming appears as one part of a broader fandom and creative ecosystem. The University of Georgia Student Board Game Club ran a gaming table, while local high school Beta Club volunteers greeted attendees and distributed event schedules. The report does not describe the specific board games, facilitation methods, learning outcomes, or attendance at the gaming table, so it should not be treated as evidence for a game program's impact. Still, the presence of a university board game club is important for GBLS because it shows how libraries can use existing local gaming expertise inside a larger festival format rather than staffing every activity internally.

FanFest also functioned as a platform for local creative production. Featured guests included graphic novelists, musicians, filmmakers, a local actress leading acting workshops, comic creators, face painting and henna artists, and a podcast wrap party for Classic City Vibes, an Athens Regional Library System podcast about local musicians, visual artists, literary artists, and other creatives. A donated merchandise door prize was tied to electronic newsletter sign-ups, connecting event excitement back to ongoing library communication.

For the larger GBLS project, Varsa's value is as a compact example of game-adjacent public-library festival design. It complements Virello's larger Southington Library ComiCon case by showing a smaller, local-talent version of the same model. In both cases, games are not isolated from library service; they sit beside cosplay, comics, music, crafts, authors, vendors, food, displays, contests, and community organizations. This matters because many GBLS programs will not be pure game programs. A board game table, video game tournament, cosplay contest, or playful demonstration may be one node in a wider cultural event that advances library goals around access, belonging, local creativity, partner visibility, and community gathering.

The limitations are clear. The article is only two pages, does not include formal evaluation, and gives little detail about planning, budget, staffing, accessibility, safety policies, marketing, or post-event outcomes beyond the attendance figure. Its usefulness lies in documenting a replicable event frame: an admission-free, all-ages FanFest that uses a partner venue, local creative networks, student volunteers, a university board game club, food, music, workshops, and library communication channels to make fandom a public-library commons.
sourceText:
Varsa: Oconee County Library Puts on Inaugural FanFest

Oconee County Library Puts on Inaugural FanFest

On April 2, 2022, the Oconee County Library, a member of the Athens Regional Library System, hosted its first-ever FanFest at the Oconee County Civic Center, located in Watkinsville, Georgia. With support from the Oconee County Library Friends, staff and volunteers hosted an admission-free day-long event that attracted over 350 participants and provided an opportunity for Oconee County residents to come together and celebrate in true “fest” style.

FanFest is a free event for all ages that brings together live music, guest authors and artists, a cosplay contest, gaming, vendors, food and more. The idea for FanFest came from James Mitchell, Branch Manager, and was created following the long-standing ComicCon tradition, but with a focus on all ages and showcasing local talent. What makes FanFest unique is that it features local authors, musicians, gaming organizations, vendors, food trucks, as well as local organizations, many of which are partners with the library system and represent the diversity of the northeast Georgia community.

The event took place at a local venue of a community partner, the Oconee County Civic Center, from 9:30 a.m. to 5:30 p.m. and included workshops, panel discussions, book signings, live music, and other activities. A portion of FanFest took place in the main hall, where vendors were situated and had room to display their books and products while conversing with attendees. Three music acts performed in the auditorium, including Rebecca Sunshine Band, Sacred Bull, and Razzi King, while food trucks and the Athens School of Arms sword demonstrations were set up on the lawn outside the Civic Center.

Flyer for the FanFest 2022 event
Local high school and university students were also involved. The University of Georgia Student Board Game Club ran a gaming table, and student volunteers from the Oconee County High School Beta Club greeted attendees and handed out event schedules.

Published by DigitalCommons@Kennesaw State University, 2022

1

Georgia Library Quarterly, Vol. 59, Iss. 3 [2022], Art. 13

Authors Will Smith (left) and Jacob Wenzka (right) posing with displays of their works

Featured guests and performers included the following:
• Joey Weiser, author of the Eisner Award– nominated Mermin series, as well as the popular graphic novel series Dragon Racer.
• Rebecca Sunshine Band, who perform fun and engaging music for kids and have been featured performers at AthFest Music and Arts Festival, Savannah Children’s Book Festival, and the Marigold Festival.
• Will Smith, creator of Legacy A.D., an upcoming animated cartoon series and comic book about the adventures of Deacon Foster, a detective in Atlanta chosen by God to battle evil and uphold justice.
• Razzi King, Reggae Dancehall musician whose work is on both Rasculture Records and Echobass Records.
• Gonzoriffic Films, Athens’ feminst underground cinema collective that has been putting out films for 20 years; and many others.
Notably, the event held two acting workshops hosted by Toi Mekhi, a local actress who made her debut in the movie Remember the Titans as a featured extra and who has gone on to do theater, commercials, and teaching. Her latest

role is in the dystopian comic adaptation of DMZ on HBO Max which was released in early 2022.
In addition to these guests, Montu Miller, Shadebeast, Dragonstar Hobbies, Blacknerdninja, Jacob Wenzka, Autumn Deoliveira, David and Owen Barnes, Marlena Frank, Will Eskrigde, Momocon, Morbid Smile, and Painted Expressions Face Paint and Henna, tabled for this event.
The event also served as a season 2 wrap party for the Classic City Vibes Podcast, produced by the Athens Regional Library System, which features animated conversations with local musicians, visual and literary artists, and other creatives. A door prize consisting of donated merchandise from podcast guests was awarded to a FanFest participant at random who signed up to receive the Oconee County Library electronic newsletter.
For more information about FanFest, contact author Jessica Varsa, Adult Services Librarian, or James Mitchell, Branch Manager, at the Oconee County library.
Jessica Varsa is adult services librarian at Oconee County Library

https://digitalcommons.kennesaw.edu/glq/vol59/iss3/13

2

Intended_Outcome.creativity_and_producti.quote: The University of Georgia Student Board Game Club ran a gaming table, and student volunteers from the Oconee County High School Beta Club greeted attendees and handed out event schedules.

In addition to these guests, Montu Miller, Shadebeast, Dragonstar Hobbies, Blacknerdninja, Jacob Wenzka, Autumn Deoliveira, David and Owen Barnes, Marlena Frank, Will Eskrigde, Momocon, Morbid Smile, and Painted Expressions Face Paint and Henna, tabled for this event.

The event also served as a season 2 wrap party for the Classic City Vibes Podcast, produced by the Athens Regional Library System, which features animated conversations with local musicians, visual and literary artists, and other creatives.

Intended_Outcome.wellness_and_resilience.quote: For more information about FanFest, contact author Jessica Varsa, Adult Services Librarian, or James Mitchell, Branch Manager, at the Oconee County library.

Intended_Outcome.cultural_stewardship_an.quote: For more information about FanFest, contact author Jessica Varsa, Adult Services Librarian, or James Mitchell, Branch Manager, at the Oconee County library.

Intended_Outcome.institutional_capacity.quote: With support from the Oconee County Library Friends, staff and volunteers hosted an admission-free day-long event that attracted over 350 participants and provided an opportunity for Oconee County residents to come together and celebrate in true “fest” style.
For more information about FanFest, contact author Jessica Varsa, Adult Services Librarian, or James Mitchell, Branch Manager, at the Oconee County library.

Intended_Outcome.not_applicable.quote: Notably, the event held two acting workshops hosted by Toi Mekhi, a local actress who made her debut in the movie Remember the Titans as a featured extra and who has gone on to do theater, commercials, and teaching.
For more information about FanFest, contact author Jessica Varsa, Adult Services Librarian, or James Mitchell, Branch Manager, at the Oconee County library.

Coding_Confidence.high.quote: Local high school and university students were also involved.

Coding_Confidence.low.quote: The idea for FanFest came from James Mitchell, Branch Manager, and was created following the long-standing ComicCon tradition, but with a focus on all ages and showcasing local talent.
